sounds are the deep, low-register pitches below approximately 70 [Hz](/wiki/Hertz "Hertz")[[1]](#cite_note-defn-tone-1) (C♯2 in [scientific pitch notation](/wiki/Scientific_pitch_notation "Scientific pitch notation")) and extending downward to include the lowest frequency humans can hear, approximately 20 Hz (E0).

Fink, Robert (2018). "Below 100 Hz: Toward a Musicology of Bass Culture". In Latour, Melinda; Fink, Robert; Wallmark, Zachary (eds.). *The Relentless Pursuit of Tone: Timbre in Popular Music*. Oxford University Press. pp. 92–93. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0199985258](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0199985258 "Special:BookSources/978-0199985258"). "Many sound engineers start talking about "sub-bass" around 70 Hz, [...]; at the bottom of the sub-bass register, around 30 Hz"

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Frequency range

This article is about frequency range. For the organ stop (also called "Soubasse"), see [Bourdon (organ pipe)](/wiki/Bourdon_(organ_pipe) "Bourdon (organ pipe)"). For the type of singer, see [Oktavist](/wiki/Oktavist "Oktavist"). For the object in mathematical topology, see [Subbase](/wiki/Subbase "Subbase"). For the layer of a paved road, see [Subbase (pavement)](/wiki/Subbase_(pavement) "Subbase (pavement)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/%28Portrait_of_Teddy_Kaye%2C_Vivien_Garry%2C_and_Arv%28in%29_Charles_Garrison%2C_Dixon%27s%2C_New_York%2C_N.Y.%2C_ca._May_1947%29_%28LOC%29_%284976467461%29.jpg/250px-%28Portrait_of_Teddy_Kaye%2C_Vivien_Garry%2C_and_Arv%28in%29_Charles_Garrison%2C_Dixon%27s%2C_New_York%2C_N.Y.%2C_ca._May_1947%29_%28LOC%29_%284976467461%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:(Portrait_of_Teddy_Kaye,_Vivien_Garry,_and_Arv(in)_Charles_Garrison,_Dixon%27s,_New_York,_N.Y.,_ca._May_1947)_(LOC)_(4976467461).jpg)

[Double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass") player [Vivien Garry](/wiki/Vivien_Garry "Vivien Garry") playing a show in New York City in 1947. The double bass is the sub-bass instrument of the orchestral strings family, as it produces the pitches in the lowest register for this family.

**Sub-bass** sounds are the deep, low-register pitches below approximately 70 [Hz](/wiki/Hertz "Hertz")[[1]](#cite_note-defn-tone-1) (C♯2 in [scientific pitch notation](/wiki/Scientific_pitch_notation "Scientific pitch notation")) and extending downward to include the lowest frequency humans can hear, approximately 20 Hz (E0).

In this range, human hearing is less sensitive, so these notes tend to be felt more than heard.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The low E-string on a [bass guitar](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar") is usually tuned to 41.2 Hz, while the lowest note on a standard [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano") is A at 27.5 Hz. [Sound reinforcement systems](/wiki/Sound_reinforcement_system "Sound reinforcement system") and [PA systems](/wiki/PA_system "PA system") often use one or more [subwoofer](/wiki/Subwoofer "Subwoofer") loudspeakers to amplify sounds in the sub-bass range. Sounds below sub-bass are [infrasound](/wiki/Infrasound "Infrasound").

## Hearing and usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sub-bass&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Hearing and usage")]

20 Hz is considered the normal low-frequency limit of human hearing[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. When pure sine waves are reproduced under ideal conditions and at very high [decibels](/wiki/Decibel "Decibel"), a human listener will be able to identify tones as low as 12 Hz (G–1).[[3]](#cite_note-3) Audio tracks known as bass tests use sub-bass frequencies which are used to test or to demonstrate the capabilities of audio equipment. High-end subwoofers can accurately reproduce sound to about 18 Hz ±2 dB.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

Sub-bass is popular in [dance music](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music"), where the low frequencies are produced by the [kick drum](/wiki/Kick_drum "Kick drum") (bass drum), the [bass guitar](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar") and electronic [synthesizers](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer") and [drum machines](/wiki/Drum_machine "Drum machine"). Particular genres such as [house music](/wiki/House_music "House music"), [drum and bass](/wiki/Drum_and_bass "Drum and bass") and [dubstep](/wiki/Dubstep "Dubstep") often feature a [bassline](/wiki/Bassline "Bassline") that consists mainly of sub-bass frequencies. Much [experimental music](/wiki/Experimental_music "Experimental music") uses sub-bass, in particular [drone music](/wiki/Drone_music "Drone music"), where the majority of the sound can often be in the sub-bass range. Often, [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") and rap songs feature prevalent sub-bass. The [pedal keyboard](/wiki/Pedal_keyboard "Pedal keyboard") range on [pipe organs](/wiki/Pipe_organ "Pipe organ") also often extends into the sub-bass range; the bottom note of a 16′ stop is typically tuned to 32 Hz (C1), a 32′ stop at 16 Hz (C0).

[ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0199985258](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0199985258 "Special:BookSources/978-0199985258"). "Many sound engineers start talking about "sub-bass" around 70 Hz, [...]; at the bottom of the sub-bass register, around 30 Hz"

[ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-486-21769-8](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-486-21769-8 "Special:BookSources/0-486-21769-8").
